text:
User: I  wanna be able to launch in channels of other workspaces directly, like I wanna be in workspace1 and use `tenex-edge  launch codex --channel 'workspace2.channel' -- but not sure how to differentiate from relative (i.e. not
  workspace1.workspace2.channel) -- maybe '#workspace2.channel'? maybe channels should have had always a / path instead so
  relative stuff could have been done more cleanly? yeah, fuck it, let's do it that way

change . to / so workspaces are always /workspace so `tenex-edge launch <agent> --channel '/workspace1/channel1/channel2'` or --channel `channel2` -- thoughts on this idea? [...] Assistant: Yes—I think slash paths are the cleaner model, provided they become the only channel-reference grammar.

```text
/workspace2/channel1/channel2   absolute
channel1/channel2               relative to current workspace root
channel2                        relative to current workspace root
@deadbeef                       opaque-ID escape hatch
```

Important decisions:

- Relative means workspace-root-relative, never active-channel-relative. Otherwise accidental nesting returns.
- Reject dotted refs outright—no compatibility aliases.
- Don’t implement filesystem semantics yet: reject `.`, `..`, `//`, and trailing `/`.
- Canonical output is always absolute: `/workspace/channel`. [...] ```text
/workspace2/channel1/channel2   absolute
channel1/channel2               relative to current workspace root
channel2                        relative to current workspace root
@deadbeef                       opaque-ID escape hatch
``` [...] - Relative means workspace-root-relative, never active-channel-relative. Otherwise accidental nesting returns. [...] - Canonical output is always absolute: `/workspace/channel`. [...] @deadbeef                       opaque-ID escape hatch
```
